question_text: A processor has one general purpose register, the Accumulator (ACC), and several special purpose registers. Load the number n to ACC LDD <address> Direct addressing. Load the contents of the location at the given address to ACC LDI <address> Indirect addressing: The address to be used is at the given address. Load the contents of this second address to ACC LDX <address> Indexed addressing. Form the address from <address> + the contents of the Index Register. Copy the contents of this calculated address to ACC LDR #n Immediate addressing. Load the number n to IX MOV <register> Move the contents of the accumulator to the given register (IX) STO <address> Store contents of ACC at the given address ADD <address> Add the contents of the given address to the ACC INC <register> Add 1 to the contents of the register (ACC or IX) CMP <address> Compare the contents of ACC with the contents of <address> JPE <address> Following a compare instruction, jump to <address> if the compare was True JPN <address> Following a compare instruction, jump to <address> if the compare was False JMP <address> Jump to the given address OUT Output to the screen the character whose ASCII value is stored in ACC END Return control to the operating system LSL #n Bits in ACC are shifted logically n places to the left. subject: A-Level Computer Science
question_text: Melinda and her friends set up a peer-to-peer network between their computers to share data. (a) Describe the key features of a peer-to-peer network. [2] (b) Describe two drawbacks to Melinda and her friends of using a peer-to-peer network. 1 2 [4] (c) Melinda connects her laptop to the internet through her router. (i) Tick ( 3 ) one box in each row to identify whether the task is performed by the router or not. Task Performed by router Not performed by router Receives packets from devices Finds the IP address of a Uniform Resource Locator (URL) Directs each packet to all devices attached to it Stores the IP and/or MAC address of all devices attached to it [2] 12 9618/11/M/J/21 (ii) Melinda mainly uses the internet to watch films and play computer games. Tick ( 3 ) one box to identify whether Melinda should connect to the router using a wired or wireless network and justify your choice. Wired Wireless Justification [3] (d) Melinda sends emails from her webmail account (email account accessed through a website). Explain whether Melinda is using the internet, or the World Wide Web (WWW), or both. [3] 13 9618/11/M/J/21 [Turn over
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) 1 mark per bullet point to max 2 ΟΔβ²§¬Δ All computers are of equal status ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Each computer provides access to resources and data // data is distributed ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Computers can communicate and share resources ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Each computer is responsible for its own security 2

4(b) 1 mark per bullet point to max 2 per drawback ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Reduced security // no central management of security ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π only as secure as the weakest computer on the network ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π each computer is at risk from viruses from other computers ΟΔβ²§¬Δ No central management of backup ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ... if the data from one computer is not backed up it is lost to all of them ΟΔβ²§¬Δ No central management of files/software ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π consistency may be difficult to maintain ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π each computer may have different software from the others ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Individual computers may respond slower ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π because they are being accessed by other computers ΟΔβ²§¬Δ In order to share files etc. all the computers involved need to be switched on ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ... so the files etc. may not be always available 4 9618/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 7 of 10 Question Answer Marks

4(c)(i) 1 mark for first 2 ticks, 1 mark for last 2 (shaded) Task Performed by router Not performed by router Receives packets from devices Ο·Τ£¬Φ Finds the IP address of a Uniform Resource Locator (URL) Ο·Τ£¬Φ Directs each packet to all devices attached to it Ο·Τ£¬Φ Stores the IP and/or MAC address of all devices attached to it Ο·Τ£¬Φ 2

4(c)(ii) 1 mark per bullet point for justification up to max 3 No mark for identification of wired/wireless Wired ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Faster connection // higher bandwidth ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π. needed as she is downloading/streaming large files ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π less time waiting / less latency / fewer delays ΟΔβ²§¬Δ More reliable / stable connection ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π is less susceptible to issues with distance/walls/interference ΟΔβ²§¬Δ More secure Wireless ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Freedom of movement ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π can move between different rooms with a mobile device and still receive/transmit data ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π no need of a physical connection ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Easily expanded if friends want to access the same network ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Less cabling / expertise is needed ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ... making the initial setup less expensive 3

4(d) 1 mark for identifying that she is using both. 1 mark per bullet point for justification ΟΔβ²§¬Δ using internet because sending data on the infrastructure ΟΔβ²§¬Δ using WWW because accessing a website (that is stored on a web server operated by the webmail) that is part of the WWW 3 9618/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 8 of 10 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Kiara has a washing machine and a refrigerator. (a) She has an embedded system in her washing machine. 5(b) 1 mark for RAM, 1 mark for ROM RAM: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Store the choices/wash program the user has entered // stores the data read from the sensors // stores the time left in the program // by example ROM: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Store the start-up instructions (for the washing cycles) 2

5(a) 1 mark for each term correctly inserted The control unit/bus transmits the signals to coordinate events based on the pulses of the ( system) clock . The data bus carries data to components, while the address bus carries the address where data is being written to or read from. The arithmetic logic unit/ALU performs mathematical operations and logical comparisons. 5

5(b) 1 mark per bullet point to max 3 per factor. max 4 overall. Number of cores: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Each core processes one instruction per clock pulse ΟΔβ²§¬Δ More/multiple cores mean that sequences of instructions can be split between them ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π and so more than one instruction is executed per clock pulse // more sequences of instructions can be run at the same time ΟΔβ²§¬Δ More cores decreases the time taken to complete task Clock speed: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Each instruction is executed on a clock pulse // one F-E cycle is run on each clock pulse ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ... so the clock speed dictates the number of instructions that can be run per second ΟΔβ²§¬Δ The faster the clock speed the more instructions can be run per second 4

5(c)(i) 1 mark per bullet point to max 2 ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Cloud storage can be free (for small quantities ) ΟΔβ²§¬Δ No need for separate (high capacity) storage devices // saves storage on existing devices ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Can access data from any computer with internet access ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Most cloud data services will have in-built backup/disaster recovery ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Security could be better ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Can easily increase capacity ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Data can be easily shared 2 9618/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 8 of 10 Question Answer Marks

5(c)(ii) 1 mark per bullet point to max 2 : ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Can only access (the cloud) with internet access ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Security may not be strong // no control over security ΟΔβ²§¬Δ There may not be any backups // no control over backups ΟΔβ²§¬Δ It can take a long time to upload/download the data ΟΔβ²§¬Δ It can be more expensive in the long term ΟΔβ²§¬Δ There could be a limit to the amount of storage unless paid for ΟΔβ²§¬Δ There could be compatibility/access issues ΟΔβ²§¬Δ There could be issues with the company offering cloud services 2

[5] 7 9618/13/M/J/21 [Turn over (b) The following table shows part of the instruction set for a processor. The processor has one general purpose register, the Accumulator (ACC), and an Index Register (IX). Instruction Explanation Opcode Operand LDM #n Immediate addressing. Load the number n to ACC LDD <address> Direct addressing. Load the contents of the location at the given address to ACC LDI <address> Indirect addressing: The address to be used is at the given address. Load the contents of this second address to ACC LDX <address> Indexed addressing. Form the address from <address> + the contents of the Index Register. Copy the contents of this calculated address to ACC LDR #n Immediate addressing. Load the number n to IX MOV <register> Move the contents of the accumulator to the given register (IX) STO <address> Store contents of ACC at the given address ADD <address> Add the contents of the given address to the ACC INC <register> Add 1 to the contents of the register (ACC or IX) CMP <address> Compare the contents of ACC with the contents of <address> JPE <address> Following a compare instruction, jump to <address> if the compare was True JPN <address> Following a compare instruction, jump to <address> if the compare was False JMP <address> Jump to the given address OUT Output to the screen the character whose ASCII value is stored in ACC END Return control to the operating system LSL #n Bits in ACC are shifted logically n places to the left. Zeros are introduced on the right hand end LSR #n Bits in ACC are shifted logically n places to the right. Zeros are introduced on the left hand end <address> can be an absolute address or a symbolic address # denotes a denary number, e.g. #123 8 9618/13/M/J/21 The current contents of the main memory and selected values from the ASCII character set are shown. question_text: A concert venue uses a program to calculate admission prices and store information about ticket sales. A number of arrays are used to store data. (i) Explain how the program could use text files to speed up the process. [2] (ii) State the characteristic of text files that allow them to be used as explained in part (a)(i) . [1] (iii) Information about ticket sales will be stored as a booking. The booking requires the following data: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ name of person booking ΟΔβ²§¬Δ number of people in the group (for example a family ticket or a school party) ΟΔβ²§¬Δ event type. Suggest how data relating to each booking may be stored in a text file. 3(a)(ii) ΟΔβ²§¬Δ The data is retained when the program is terminated / after the computer is switched off // data is stored permanently // non-volatile storage 1

3(a)(iii) One mark per bullet point: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Data items are combined to form a single string / saved as a single line in the file ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Data items are separated by a special character // make each data item a fixed length ALTERNATIVE: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Convert all data items / 'number of people' to strings ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Consecutive / each line stores a separate data item 2 9618/21 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 8 of 16 Question Answer Marks

3(b) PROCEDURE Preview (ThisFile : STRING) DECLARE LineNum : INTEGER DECLARE ThisLine : STRING OPENFILE ThisFile FOR READ IF EOF(ThisFile) THEN OUTPUT ΟΔβ²§≈™Warning MessageΟΔβ²§¬ù ELSE LineNum ΟΔβÄ†¬ê 1 WHILE LineNum < 6 AND NOT EOF(ThisFile) READFILE Thisfile, ThisLine OUTPUT ThisLine LineNum ΟΔβÄ†¬ê LineNum + 1 ENDWHILE ENDIF CLOSEFILE ThisFile ENDPROCEDURE Marks as follows: 1 Procedure heading (including parameter) and ending 2 File OPEN and subsequently CLOSE 3 Check if file is empty and output a warning message if it is 4 Conditional Loop 5 Output line (including blank lines) and read next line in a loop 5 9618/21 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 9 of 16 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Computer Science
question_text: A program is needed to take a string containing a full name and produce a new string of initials. Some words in the full name will be ignored. For example, "the", "and", "of", "for" and "to" may all be ignored. Each letter of the abbreviated string must be upper case. For example: Full name Initials Integrated Development Environment IDE The American Standard Code for Information Interchange ASCII The programmer has decided to use a global variable FNString of type STRING to store the full name. It is assumed that: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ words in the full name string are separated by a single space character ΟΔβ²§¬Δ space characters will not occur at the beginning or the end of the full name string ΟΔβ²§¬Δ the full name string contains at least one word. The programmer has started to define program modules as follows: Module Description GetStart() ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Called with an INTEGER as a parameter, representing the number of a word in FNString . ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Returns the character start position of that word in FNString or returns ΟΔβ²§βÄ€1 if that word does not exist ΟΔβ²§¬Δ For example: if FNString contains the string "hot and cold" , GetStart(3) returns
mark_scheme_text:
7(a) FUNCTION GetStart (WordNum : INTEGER) RETURNS INTEGER DECLARE Index, ThisPos, NumFound : INTEGER DECLARE ThisChar : Char CONSTANT SPACECHAR = ' ' Index ΟΔβÄ†¬ê -1 Numfound ΟΔβÄ†¬ê 0 ThisPos ΟΔβÄ†¬ê 1 IF WordNum = 1 THEN // if looking for word 1... Index ΟΔβÄ†¬ê 1 // Word 1 always starts at index // position 1 ELSE // Otherwise start counting spaces... WHILE ThisPos <= LENGTH(FNString) AND Index = -1 ThisChar ΟΔβÄ†¬ê MID(FNString, ThisPos, 1) IF ThisChar = SPACECHAR THEN NumFound ΟΔβÄ†¬ê NumFound + 1 IF NumFound = WordNum - 1 THEN Index ΟΔβÄ†¬ê ThisPos + 1 // the start of the // required word ENDIF ENDIF ThisPos ΟΔβÄ†¬ê ThisPos + 1 ENDWHILE ENDIF RETURN Index ENDFUNCTION 1 mark for each of the following: 1 Function heading, including return type and function end 2 Loop counting spaces until word found or end of FNString 3 extract a character from FNString in a loop 4 compare with SPACECHAR and increment count if equal in a loop 5 compare count with WordNum - 1 (depending on initialisation value) in a loop 6 if equal then set flag or Index to ThisPos + 1 in a loop 7 Return Index (correctly in all cases / following a reasonable attempt) 8 Works for special case when looking for word 1 Note: Max 7 marks 7 9618/21 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 14 of 16 Question Answer Marks

7(b) Marks awarded for any reference to each of the following steps of the algorithm: 1 Mention of variable for use as array index 2 Use of a loop (to check through the array) 3 If word is the same as the current array element then return FALSE / set flag 4 If word not already in array, loop to find unused element (second loop) 5 Store word in unused element and return TRUE , otherwise return FALSE VARIATION: 1 Mention of variable for use as array index 2 Use of a loop (to check through the array) 3 Save index of (first) unused element found 4 If word is the same as the current array element then return FALSE / set flag 5 If word not already in array and unused element available, store word in unused element and return TRUE otherwise return FALSE Note: Max 4 marks 4 9618/21 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 15 of 16 Question Answer Marks

7(c) FUNCTION GetWord (Index : INTEGER) RETURNS STRING DECLARE NextWord : STRING DECLARE Done : BOOLEAN DECLARE ThisChar : CHAR DECLARE Index : INTEGER CONSTANT SPACECHAR = ' ' NextWord ΟΔβÄ†¬ê "" Done ΟΔβÄ†¬ê FALSE REPEAT ThisChar ΟΔβÄ†¬ê MID(FNString, Index, 1) IF ThisChar <> SPACECHAR THEN NextWord ΟΔβÄ†¬ê NextWord & ThisChar // build up NextWord ENDIF IF ThisChar = SPACECHAR OR Index = LENGTH(FNString) THEN Done ΟΔβÄ†¬ê TRUE ENDIF Index ΟΔβÄ†¬ê Index + 1 UNTIL Done = TRUE RETURN NextWord ENDFUNCTION 1 mark for each of the following: 1 Conditional loop 2 Extract char from FNString and compare with SPACECHAR in a loop 3 Concatenate with NextWord if not SPACECHAR in a loop 4 Exit loop when SPACECHAR encountered or when end of FNString reached 5 Return NextWord (after reasonable attempt at forming, and must have been initialised)

question_text: A program is needed to take a string containing a full name and produce a new string of initials. Some words in the full name will be ignored. For example, "the", "and", "of", "for" and "to" may all be ignored. Each letter of the abbreviated string must be upper case. The programmer has started to define program modules as follows: Module Description GetStart() ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Called with an INTEGER as a parameter, representing the number of a word in FNString . ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Returns the character start position of that word in FNString or returns ΟΔβ²§βÄ€1 if that word does not exist ΟΔβ²§¬Δ For example: if FNString contains the string "hot and cold" , GetStart(3) returns

7(a) FUNCTION GetStart (WordNum : INTEGER) RETURNS INTEGER DECLARE Index, ThisPos, NumFound : INTEGER DECLARE ThisChar : Char CONSTANT SPACECHAR = ' ' Index ΟΔβÄ†¬ê -1 Numfound ΟΔβÄ†¬ê 0 ThisPos ΟΔβÄ†¬ê 1 IF WordNum = 1 THEN // if looking for word 1... Index ΟΔβÄ†¬ê 1 // Word 1 always starts at index // position 1 ELSE // Otherwise start counting spaces... WHILE ThisPos <= LENGTH(FNString) AND Index = -1 ThisChar ΟΔβÄ†¬ê MID(FNString, ThisPos, 1) IF ThisChar = SPACECHAR THEN NumFound ΟΔβÄ†¬ê NumFound + 1 IF NumFound = WordNum - 1 THEN Index ΟΔβÄ†¬ê ThisPos + 1 // the start of the // required word ENDIF ENDIF ThisPos ΟΔβÄ†¬ê ThisPos + 1 ENDWHILE ENDIF RETURN Index ENDFUNCTION 1 mark for each of the following: 1 Function heading, including return type and function end 2 Loop counting spaces until word found or end of FNString 3 extract a character from FNString in a loop 4 compare with SPACECHAR and increment count if equal in a loop 5 compare count with WordNum - 1 (depending on initialisation value) in a loop 6 if equal then set flag or Index to ThisPos + 1 in a loop 7 Return Index (correctly in all cases / following a reasonable attempt) 8 Works for special case when looking for word 1 Note: Max 7 marks 7 9618/23 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 14 of 16 Question Answer Marks

7(c) FUNCTION GetWord (Index : INTEGER) RETURNS STRING DECLARE NextWord : STRING DECLARE Done : BOOLEAN DECLARE ThisChar : CHAR DECLARE Index : INTEGER CONSTANT SPACECHAR = ' ' NextWord ΟΔβÄ†¬ê "" Done ΟΔβÄ†¬ê FALSE REPEAT ThisChar ΟΔβÄ†¬ê MID(FNString, Index, 1) IF ThisChar <> SPACECHAR THEN NextWord ΟΔβÄ†¬ê NextWord & ThisChar // build up NextWord ENDIF IF ThisChar = SPACECHAR OR Index = LENGTH(FNString) THEN Done ΟΔβÄ†¬ê TRUE ENDIF Index ΟΔβÄ†¬ê Index + 1 UNTIL Done = TRUE RETURN NextWord ENDFUNCTION 1 mark for each of the following: 1 Conditional loop 2 Extract char from FNString and compare with SPACECHAR in a loop 3 Concatenate with NextWord if not SPACECHAR in a loop 4 Exit loop when SPACECHAR encountered or when end of FNString reached 5 Return NextWord (after reasonable attempt at forming, and must have been initialised)

question_text: The following data items will be recorded each time a student successfully logs on to the school network: Data item Example data Student ID "CJL404" Host ID "Lib01" Time and date "08:30, June 01, 2021" The Student ID is six characters long. The other two data items are of variable length. A single string will be formed by concatenating the three data items. A separator character will need to be inserted between items two and three. For example: "CJL404Lib01<separator>08:30, June 01, 2021" Each string represents one log entry. A programmer decides to store the concatenated strings in a 1D array LogArray that contains 2000 elements. Unused array elements will contain an empty string. (a) Suggest a suitable separator character and give a reason for your choice. Character Reason [2] (b) The choice of data structure was made during one stage of the program development life cycle. Identify this stage. [1] 13 9618/21/O/N/21 [Turn over (c) A function LogEvents() will: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ take a Student ID as a parameter ΟΔβ²§¬Δ for each element in the array that matches the Student ID parameter: ΟΔβÄî¬Π add the value of the array element to the existing text file LogFile ΟΔβÄî¬Π assign an empty string to the array element ΟΔβ²§¬Δ count the number of lines added to the file ΟΔβ²§¬Δ return this count. Write pseudocode for the function LogEvents() . [7] 14 9618/21/O/N/21
mark_scheme_text:

5(b) Design 1 9618/21 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2021 Page 8 of 11 Question Answer Marks

5(c) FUNCTION LogEvents(StudentID : STRING) RETURNS INTEGER DECLARE FileData : STRING DECLARE Index, Count : INTEGER CONSTANT LogFile = "LogFile" Count ΟΔβÄ†¬ê 0 OPENFILE LogFile FOR APPEND FOR Index ΟΔβÄ†¬ê 1 TO 2000 FileData ΟΔβÄ†¬ê LogArray[Index] IF LEFT(FileData, 6) = StudentID THEN WRITEFILE (LogFile, FileData) //brackets optional Count ΟΔβÄ†¬ê Count + 1 LogArray[Index] ΟΔβÄ†¬ê "" // clear the element ENDIF NEXT Index CLOSEFILE LogFile RETURN Count ENDFUNCTION 1 mark for each of the following: 1 Function heading and ending including parameter and return type 2 OPEN file LogFile for APPEND and subsequent CLOSE 3 Loop for 2000 iterations 4 Extract first 6 characters from array element in a loop 5 Compare first 6 characters with parameter in a loop 6 If equal: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ write whole array element string to file and ΟΔβ²§¬Δ increment Count and ΟΔβ²§¬Δ clear array element in a loop 7 Return Count (must have been declared and initialised) 7 9618/21 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2021 Page 9 of 11 Question Answer Marks

An example summary output is as follows: File Marketing contained 54 employee details 52 employee sets of details were written to file NewMarketingList (a) Write pseudocode for the procedure MakeNewFile() . 13 9618/22/O/N/21 [Turn over [7] 14 9618/22/O/N/21 (b) An alternative format could be used for storing the data. A text file will still be used. (i) Describe the alternative format. [1] (ii) State one advantage and one disadvantage of the alternative format. Advantage Disadvantage [2] 15 9618/22/O/N/21 [Turn over BLANK PAGE 16 9618/22/O/N/21 A mobile phone has a touchscreen. The screen is represented by a grid, divided into 800 rows and 1280 columns. The grid is represented by a 2D array Screen of type INTEGER . An array element will be set to 0 unless the user touches that part of the screen. Many array elements will set to 1 by a single touch of a finger or a stylus. The following diagram shows a simplified touchscreen. The dark line represents a touch on the screen. All grid elements that are wholly or partly inside the outline will be set to 1. These elements are shaded. The element shaded in black represents the centre point of the touch. 11 A program is needed to find the coordinates (the row and column) of the centre point. The centre point on the diagram shown is row 6, column 11. Assume: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ the user may only touch one area at a time ΟΔβ²§¬Δ screen rotation does not affect the touchscreen. The programmer has decided to use global values CentreRow and CentreCol as coordinate values for the centre point. The programmer has started to define program modules as follows: Module Description FirstRowSet() ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Searches for the first row that has an array element set to 1 ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Returns the index of that row (1 is the first row) ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Returns ΟΔΥÜβÄô1 if there are no elements set to
mark_scheme_text:
6(a) FUNCTION FirstRowSet() RETURNS INTEGER DECLARE Row, Col : INTEGER DECLARE Found : BOOLEAN // array is 1280 ΟÉβÄî 800 Row ΟΔβÄ†¬ê 1 Found ΟΔβÄ†¬ê FALSE WHILE Row <= 800 AND Found = FALSE // top to bottom Col ΟΔβÄ†¬ê 1 WHILE Col <= 1280 AND Found = FALSE // left to right IF Screen[Row,Col] = 1 THEN Found ΟΔβÄ†¬ê TRUE // end function as soon as first // found ENDIF Col ΟΔβÄ†¬ê Col + 1 ENDWHILE Row ΟΔβÄ†¬ê Row + 1 ENDWHILE IF Found = FALSE THEN // nothing found Row ΟΔβÄ†¬ê 0 ENDIF RETURN Row - 1 ENDFUNCTION Mark as follows: 1 Function heading and ending and return type 2 (Conditional) outer loop 1 to 800 (row) 3 (Conditional) inner loop 1 to 1280 // 1280 to 1 (column) 4 Reference Screen element and test for = 1 // <> 0 5 and if true save row number and exit loops 6 Increment index variables in both inner and outer loop 7 Return Row number or ΟΔΥÜβÄô 1, following a reasonable attempt 7

6(b) One mark for: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ (A flag is used to) exit the loops // iteration is terminated ΟΔβ²§¬Δ as soon as a Screen element with value 1 is found 2 9618/22 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2021 Page 10 of 10 Question Answer Marks

6(c)(i) One mark for: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Parameter(s) need to be passed to the module to identify the type of search ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Search algorithm is controlled by (global) variables / parameters Alternative: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ The search algorithms from the original modules are included in the new module ΟΔβ²§¬Δ The new module needs to return / store the four values (the results of the four searches) 2

6(c)(ii) One mark for advantage and one for disadvantage: Advantage: (max 1) ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Only have to change one module if specification changes ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Less repetitive code / fewer lines of code ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Aids re-usability Disadvantage: (max 1) ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Single module more complex / more error prone / more difficult to debug ... ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Single module cannot be split among programmers / teams Max 2 2

6(d) PROCEDURE GetCentre () DECLARE StartRow, EndRow, StartCol, EndCol : INTEGER StartRow ΟΔβÄ†¬ê FirstRowSet() IF StartRow = -1 THEN CentreRow ΟΔβÄ†¬ê -1 // no 'touch' detected ELSE EndRow ΟΔβÄ†¬ê LastRowSet() StartCol ΟΔβÄ†¬ê FirstColSet() EndCol ΟΔβÄ†¬ê LastColSet() CentreRow ΟΔβÄ†¬ê INT((StartRow + EndRow)/2) CentreCol ΟΔβÄ†¬ê INT((StartCol + EndCol)/2) ENDIF ENDPROCEDURE Mark as follows: 1 Call <any Set function> and check for -1 // check for no element set 2 ...and if so set CentreRow to ΟΔβ²§βÄ€1 3 Call all 4 Set functions to get 'extremity' values 4 Calculate centre row and centre column 5 Use of INT() function or DIV operator on values from MP4 6 Assign calculated values to CentreRow and CentreCol Note: Max 5 if procedure heading and ending missing or incorrect (ignore array if passed as a parameter) or any local variables are undefined or of incorrect type 6

subject: A-Level Computer Science
question_text: The following data items will be recorded each time a student successfully logs on to the school network: Data item Example data Student ID "CJL404" Host ID "Lib01" Time and date "08:30, June 01, 2021" The Student ID is six characters long. The other two data items are of variable length. A single string will be formed by concatenating the three data items. A separator character will need to be inserted between items two and three. For example: "CJL404Lib01<separator>08:30, June 01, 2021" Each string represents one log entry. A programmer decides to store the concatenated strings in a 1D array LogArray that contains 2000 elements. Unused array elements will contain an empty string. (a) Suggest a suitable separator character and give a reason for your choice. Character Reason [2] (b) The choice of data structure was made during one stage of the program development life cycle. Identify this stage. [1] 13 9618/23/O/N/21 [Turn over (c) A function LogEvents() will: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ take a Student ID as a parameter ΟΔβ²§¬Δ for each element in the array that matches the Student ID parameter: ΟΔβÄî¬Π add the value of the array element to the existing text file LogFile ΟΔβÄî¬Π assign an empty string to the array element ΟΔβ²§¬Δ count the number of lines added to the file ΟΔβ²§¬Δ return this count. Write pseudocode for the function LogEvents() . [7] 14 9618/23/O/N/21
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) One mark for the character and one for the corresponding reason. ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Character: Any except alphabetic, numeric, ',' ':' or space ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Reason: character doesn't occur in data to be recorded 2

5(b) Design 1 9618/23 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2021 Page 8 of 11 Question Answer Marks

5(c) FUNCTION LogEvents(StudentID : STRING) RETURNS INTEGER DECLARE FileData : STRING DECLARE Index, Count : INTEGER CONSTANT LogFile = "LogFile" Count ΟΔβÄ†¬ê 0 OPENFILE LogFile FOR APPEND FOR Index ΟΔβÄ†¬ê 1 TO 2000 FileData ΟΔβÄ†¬ê LogArray[Index] IF LEFT(FileData, 6) = StudentID THEN WRITEFILE (LogFile, FileData) //brackets optional Count ΟΔβÄ†¬ê Count + 1 LogArray[Index] ΟΔβÄ†¬ê "" // clear the element ENDIF NEXT Index CLOSEFILE LogFile RETURN Count ENDFUNCTION 1 mark for each of the following: 1 Function heading and ending including parameter and return type 2 OPEN file LogFile for APPEND and subsequent CLOSE 3 Loop for 2000 iterations 4 Extract first 6 characters from array element in a loop 5 Compare first 6 characters with parameter in a loop 6 If equal: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ write whole array element string to file and ΟΔβ²§¬Δ increment Count and ΟΔβ²§¬Δ clear array element in a loop 7 Return Count (must have been declared and initialised) 7 9618/23 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2021 Page 9 of 11 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Computer Science
question_text: Explain how packet switching is used to transfer messages across the internet. [5] (b) Outline the function of a router in packet switching. [3] 8 9618/31/O/N/21
mark_scheme_text:
6(a) One mark for each correct marking point (Max 5) ΟΔβ²§¬Δ A large message is divided up into a group of smaller chunks of the same size called packets ΟΔβ²§¬Δ The packet has a header and a payload ΟΔβ²§¬Δ The header contains a source IP address, destination IP address (and sequence number) ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Each packet is dispatched independently ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π and may travel along different routes / paths ΟΔβ²§¬Δ The packets may arrive out of order ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π and are reassembled into the original message at the destination ΟΔβ²§¬Δ If packets are missing / corrupted a re-transmission request is sent. 5

6(b) One mark for each correct marking point (Max 3) ΟΔβ²§¬Δ The router examines the packetΟΔβ²§β³Δs header ΟΔβ²§¬Δ It reads the IP address of the destination (from the packet header) ΟΔβ²§¬Δ A router has access to a routing table ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Πcontaining information about, e.g., available hops / netmask / gateway used ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π and the status of the routes along the route ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π the router decides on the next hop / best route ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π and sends the packet on its next hop. 3 9618/31 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2021 Page 7 of 10 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Computer Science
question_text: State three essential features of recursion . 1 2 3 [3] (b) Explain the reasons why a stack is a suitable Abstract Data Type (ADT) to implement recursion. [3] (c) Identify two ADTs other than a stack. 1 2 [2] 14 9618/31/O/N/21 (d) The function StackFull() checks whether a stack is full. The function uses the variable TopOfStack to represent the pointer to the most recent position used on the stack, and the variable Max to represent the maximum size of the stack. Assume TopOfStack and Max are global variables. FUNCTION StackFull() RETURNS BOOLEAN IF TopOfStack = Max THEN RETURN TRUE ELSE RETURN FALSE ENDIF ENDFUNCTION An algorithm AddInteger is required to add a new integer data element to a stack. The stack is implemented as an array ArrayStack . The function AddInteger() calls StackFull() and returns an appropriate message. Complete the pseudocode for the function AddInteger() . mark_scheme_text:
10(a) One mark for each correct marking point (Max 3) ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Must have a base case/stopping condition ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Must have a general case ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π which calls itself (recursively) // Defined in terms of itself ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π which changes its state and moves towards the base case Unwinding can occur once the base case is reached. 3

10(b) One mark for each correct marking point (Max 3) ΟΔβ²§¬Δ A stack is a LIFO data structure ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Each recursive call is pushed onto the stack ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π. and is then popped as the function ends ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Enables backtracking/unwinding ΟΔβ²§¬Π to maintain the required order. 3

10(c) One mark for each marking point (Max 2) ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Linked List ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Queue Binary Tree 2

10(d) One mark for each marking point (Max 5) ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Checking if stack is full / empty using IF ΟΔβ²§¬Π THEN ΟΔβ²§¬Π (ELSE) ΟΔβ²§¬Π ENDIF ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π correctly using StackFull() function ΟΔβ²§¬Δ RETURN suitable message if stack is full ΟΔβ²§¬Δ RETURN message if space available on stack ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Incrementing TopOfStack pointer if space available ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Assigning new data using correct NewInteger variable ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π to correct the array element in ArrayStack[] array. Example algorithm FUNCTION AddInteger(NewInteger : INTEGER) RETURNS STRING IF StackFull() THEN RETURN "The stack is full" ELSE TopOfStack ΟΔβÄ†¬ê TopOfStack + 1 ArrayStack[TopOfStack] ΟΔβÄ†¬ê NewInteger RETURN "Item added" ENDIF ENDFUNCTION 5

subject: A-Level Computer Science
question_text: A computer has hardware and software. (a) The hardware includes different types of memory. (i) Complete the description of computer memory. Random Access Memory (RAM) and Read Only Memory (ROM) are both examples of memory. One item that is stored in RAM is . One item that is stored in ROM is . RAM can be either Static RAM (SRAM) or Dynamic RAM (DRAM). SRAM uses transistors arranged as . DRAM uses transistors and . [5] (ii) Explain the difference between Programmable ROM (PROM), Erasable Programmable ROM (EPROM) and Electrically Erasable Programmable ROM (EEPROM). [3] 4 9618/11/M/J/22 (b) A magnetic hard disk is used to store data on the computer. Describe the principal operations of a magnetic hard disk. [5] 5 9618/11/M/J/22 [Turn over (c) Computers consist of logic gates. (i) Complete the table by writing one set of values (input 1 and input 2) for each gate that will give the output 1. Gate Input Description Security measure [6] 7 9618/11/M/J/22 [Turn over
mark_scheme_text:
2(a)(i) 1 mark for each correct term. Random Access Memory (RAM) and Read Only Memory (ROM) are both examples of primary memory. One item that is stored in RAM is currently running software/data/part of OS . One item that is stored in ROM is the start-up/boot-up instructions/BIOS . RAM can be either Static RAM (SRAM) or Dynamic RAM (DRAM). SRAM uses transistors arranged as flip-flops/latches. DRAM uses transistors and capacitors . 5

2(a)(ii) 1 mark per bullet point to max 3 Ο·βÄö¬Ζ PROM can be set once, EPROM and EEPROM can be overwritten multiple times. Ο·βÄö¬Ζ EPROM needs to be removed from device EEPROM can be erased in situ. Ο·βÄö¬Ζ EPROM and can be erased using UV light, EEPROM can be erased using voltage // is flash storage . Ο·βÄö¬Ζ EPROM must be entirely erased before rewriting, EEPROM does not have to be entirely erased before rewriting. 3 9618/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 4 of 8 Question Answer Marks

2(b) 1 mark per bullet point to max 5 Ο·βÄö¬Ζ The hard disk has (one or more) platter/plate/disk Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Each surface of the platter/disk is (ferrous oxide which is) capable of being magnetised Ο·βÄö¬Ζ The platters/disks are mounted on a (central) spindle Ο·βÄö¬Ζ The entire mechanism is contained inside a sealed (aluminium) box. Ο·βÄö¬Ζ The disks are rotated (at high-speed) Ο·βÄö¬Ζ (Each surface of the disk) has a read/write head mounted on an arm (positioned just above the surface) Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Electronic circuits control the movement of the arm (and hence the heads) Ο·βÄö¬Ζ The surface of the platter/disk is divided into concentric tracks / circles Ο·βÄö¬Ζ The surface of the platter/disk is divided into sectors Ο·βÄö¬Ζ One track in one sector is the basic unit of storage called a block Ο·βÄö¬Ζ The data is encoded as a magnetic pattern for each block Ο·βÄö¬Ζ When writing to disk, a variation in the current in the head produces a variation in magnetic field on the disk Ο·βÄö¬Ζ When reading from disk, a variation in magnetic field produces a variation in current through the head 5

2(c)(i) 1 mark for each correct row. Gate Input 1 Input 2 AND 1 1 NAND 0 0 1 0 1 0 XOR 0 1 1 0 NOR 0 0 4

2(c)(ii) 1 mark per bullet point Ο·βÄö¬Ζ A NAND B Ο·βÄö¬Ζ B XOR C Ο·βÄö¬Ζ OR ( A NAND B ) OR ( B XOR C ) 3 9618/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 5 of 8 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Computer Science
question_text: A teacher is writing examination papers on a laptop computer. The computer is connected to the internet. The teacher is concerned about the security and privacy of the papers. (a) State the difference between the security of data and the privacy of data. [1] (b) Identify and describe two threats to the data. Identify one security measure to protect against each threat. Each security measure must be different. Threat
mark_scheme_text:
3(a) Security prevents against loss while privacy prevents unauthorised access 1

3(b) 1 mark for identifying threat, 1 mark for description, 1 mark for security measure ( times 2 ) e.g. Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Malware Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Malicious software that replicates and can delete/damage the examination papers Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Install and run anti-malware Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Hacker/unauthorised access Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Illegal access in order to delete/damage the examination papers Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Use a firewall // strong passwords Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Spyware Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Software installed on the computer without the teacherΟΔβ²§β³Δs knowledge which records keystrokes and sends the data gathered about the examination papers to a third party Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Use a firewall / install and run anti-spyware / use a virtual (onscreen) keyboard 6 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Computer Science
question_text: A teacher uses a relational database, MARKS , to store data about students and their test marks. The database has the following structure: STUDENT(StudentID, FirstName, LastName) TEST(TestID, Description, TotalMarks) STUDENT_TEST(StudentID, TestID, Mark) (a) Describe the advantages of using a relational database compared to a file-based approach. [4] (b) Give the highest level of Normal Form (NF) the database MARKS is in and justify your choice. Normal Form Justification [3] 8 9618/11/M/J/22 (c) (i) Sample data to be stored in the table STUDENT_TEST is shown. StudentID TestID Mark
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) 1 mark per bullet point to max 4 e.g. Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Reduced data redundancy // less repeated data Ο·βÄö¬Ζ ΟΔβ²§¬Πbecause each item of data is only stored once Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Maintains data consistency // improves data integrity Ο·βÄö¬Ζ ΟΔβ²§¬Πchanges in one table will automatically update in another Ο·βÄö¬Ζ ... linked data cannot be entered differently in two tables Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Program-data independence Ο·βÄö¬Ζ ΟΔβ²§¬Πchanges to the data do not require programs to be re-written Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Complex queries are easier to run Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Can provide different views Ο·βÄö¬Ζ ΟΔβ²§¬Π.so users can only see specific aspects of the database 4

4(b) 1 mark for 3NF 1 mark per bullet for justification to max 2 Ο·βÄö¬Ζ There are no repeated attributes // it is already in 2NF Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Each field is fully dependent on the corresponding primary key // no partial dependencies Ο·βÄö¬Ζ No transitive dependencies 3 9618/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 6 of 8 Question Answer Marks

4(c)(i) 1 mark per bullet point Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Create table, table name, opening and closing brackets Ο·βÄö¬Ζ StudentID and Mark as integer Ο·βÄö¬Ζ TestID as Varchar Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Primary key correctly set up Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Foreign keys correctly set up e.g. CREATE TABLE STUDENT_TEST ( StudentId INTEGER, TestID VARCHAR, Mark INTEGER, PRIMARY KEY(StudentID,TestID), FOREIGN KEY(TestID) REFERENCES TEST(TestID), FOREIGN KEY(StudentID) REFERENCES STUDENT(StudentID) ); 5

4(c)(ii) 1 mark for each point Ο·βÄö¬Ζ AVG(Mark) Ο·βÄö¬Ζ SELECT and FROM STUDENT_TEST Ο·βÄö¬Ζ WHERE clause e.g. SELECT AVG(Mark) FROM STUDENT_TEST WHERE TestID = "A7"; 3

4(d) 1 mark per bullet point to max 3 for validation e.g. Ο·βÄö¬Ζ range check to make sure it is between 0 and max marks Ο·βÄö¬Ζ presence check to make sure a mark is entered Ο·βÄö¬Ζ type check to make sure an integer value is entered 1 mark per bullet point to max 2 for verification e.g. Ο·βÄö¬Ζ double entry - enter the mark twice and the computer compares them Ο·βÄö¬Ζ visual check ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ manually compare the mark entered with the mark on the input document 4 9618/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 7 of 8 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Computer Science
question_text: A programmer uses an Integrated Development Environment (IDE) to develop a program. (a) Draw one line from each IDE feature to its correct description. subject: A-Level Computer Science
question_text: A computer system is designed using the basic Von Neumann model. (i) Describe the role of the registers in the Fetch-Execute (F-E) cycle. [5] (ii) Describe when interrupts are detected in the F-E cycle and how the interrupts are handled. Detected Handled [5] 13 9618/11/M/J/22 [Turn over (b) Identify one factor that can affect the performance of the computer system and state how it impacts the performance. Factor Impact [2] Question 6 continues on the next page. 14 9618/11/M/J/22 (c) The table shows part of the instruction set for a processor. The processor has one general purpose register, the Accumulator (ACC). Instruction Explanation Opcode Operand AND #n Bitwise AND operation of the contents of ACC with the operand XOR #n Bitwise XOR operation of the contents of ACC with the operand OR #n Bitwise OR operation of the contents of ACC with the operand LSL #n Bits in ACC are shifted logically n places to the left. Zeros are introduced on the right hand end LSR #n Bits in ACC are shifted logically n places to the right. Zeros are introduced on the left hand end # denotes a denary number, e.g. #123 (i) Complete the register to show the result after the instruction AND #2 is executed. Register before: 0 1 1 0 1 1 0
mark_scheme_text:
6(a)(i) 1 mark per bullet point to max 5 Ο·βÄö¬Ζ The Program Counter (PC) holds the address of the next instruction ΟΔβ²§¬Π Ο·βÄö¬Ζ ΟΔβ²§¬Πand the contents are incremented / changed to the next address each cycle Ο·βÄö¬Ζ The Memory Address Register (MAR) holds the address to fetch the data (from the PC) Ο·βÄö¬Ζ The Memory Data Register (MDR) holds the data at the address in MAR Ο·βÄö¬Ζ The instruction is transferred to Current Instruction Register (CIR) for decoding and execution 5

6(a)(ii) 1 mark for detection Ο·βÄö¬Ζ At the start/end of a FE cycle 1 mark for handling to max 4 Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Priority is checked Ο·βÄö¬Ζ If lower priority than current process continue with F-E cycle Ο·βÄö¬Ζ If higher priority than current process ΟΔβ²§¬Π Ο·βÄö¬Ζ ΟΔβ²§¬Π state of current process is / registers are stored on stack Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Location / type of interrupt identified... Ο·βÄö¬Ζ ΟΔβ²§¬Πappropriate ISR is called to handle the interrupt Ο·βÄö¬Ζ When ISR finished, check for further interrupts (of high priority) / return to step 1 Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Otherwise load data from stack and continue with process 5

6(b) 1 mark for factor 1 mark for impact e.g. Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Clock speedΟΔβ²§¬Π Ο·βÄö¬Ζ ΟΔβ²§¬Πhigher clock speed means more FE cycles per second Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Number of coresΟΔβ²§¬Π Ο·βÄö¬Ζ ΟΔβ²§¬Πmeans more instructions can be carried out simultaneously Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Bus width ΟΔβ²§¬Π Ο·βÄö¬Ζ ΟΔβ²§¬Πallows the transfer of more data each time // allows more memory locations to be directly accessed Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Cache ΟΔβ²§¬Π Ο·βÄö¬Ζ ΟΔβ²§¬Π the higher capacity the more frequently used instructions it can store for fast access 2

6(c)(i) 0000 0000 1

6(c)(ii) 0110 1101 1

6(c)(iii) 1101 0000 1

[3] 10 9618/12/M/J/22 (e) A Database Management System (DBMS) is used to create and manipulate the database. Complete the descriptions of the features and tools found in a DBMS using the given terms. Not all terms will be used. Boolean data dictionary data redundancy field names input interface logical schema normalisation operating system output primary keys query structure A DBMS provides data management. This includes the development of a that stores information about the data stored, such as and . The uses methods, such as an E-R diagram, to show the structure of the database and its relationships. mark_scheme_text:
5(a) 1 mark for each correct relationship 2

5(b) 1 mark per point Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Neither key uniquely identifies each tuple by itself Ο·βÄö¬Ζ One actor cannot appear in the same film twice so together they are unique 2

5(d) 1 mark per point Ο·βÄö¬Ζ COUNT and correct fieldname Ο·βÄö¬Ζ SELECT and FROM statements, including the table name in FROM Ο·βÄö¬Ζ WHERE statement e.g. SELECT COUNT(FilmID) FROM FILM_FACT WHERE ReleaseDate >= #01/01/2022# AND ReleaseDate <= #31/01/2022#; // WHERE ReleaseDate BETWEEN #01/01/2022# AND #31/01/2022#; // WHERE ReleaseDate = ΟΔβ²§≈™January 2022ΟΔβ²§¬ù; 3 ACTOR FILM_FACT FILM_ACTOR 9618/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 7 of 9 Question Answer Marks

5(e) 1 mark for each correctly completed term Ο·βÄö¬Ζ data dictionary Ο·βÄö¬Ζ field names // primary keys Ο·βÄö¬Ζ primary keys //field names Ο·βÄö¬Ζ logical schema Ο·βÄö¬Ζ query Ο·βÄö¬Ζ interface A DBMS provides data management. This includes the development of a data dictionary that stores information about the data stored, such as field names and primary keys . The logical schema uses methods such as an E-R diagram to show the structure of the database and its relationships. The query processor allows a user to perform searches to find specific data. The DBMS also provides a developer interface that allows the user to create tables, forms and reports. 6 Question Answer Marks

mark_scheme_text:

question_text: A program allows a user to save passwords used to login to websites. A stored password is inserted automatically when the user logs into the corresponding website. A student is developing a program to generate a password. The password will be of a fixed format, consisting of three groups of four alphanumeric characters. The groups are separated by the hyphen character '-'. An example of a password is: "FxAf-3haV-Tq49" A global 2D array Secret of type STRING stores the passwords together with the website domain name where they are used. Secret contains 1000 elements organised as 500 rows by 2 columns. Unused elements contain the empty string ( "" ). These may occur anywhere in the array. An example of a part of the array is: Array element Value Secret[27, 1] " Secret[27, 2] " Ο·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈Η " Secret[28, 1] " Secret[28, 2] " Ο·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈Η " Note: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ For security, passwords are stored in an encrypted form, shown as " Ο·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈ΗΟ·βÄö≈Η " in the example. ΟΔβ²§¬Δ The passwords cannot be used without being decrypted. ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Assume that the encrypted form of a password will not be an empty string. ΟΔβ²§¬Δ The passwords cannot be used without being decrypted. ΟΔβ²§¬Δ You may assume that the encrypted form of a password will NOT be an empty string. The programmer has started to define program modules as follows: Module Description Exists() ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Takes two parameters: ΟΔβÄîβÄΙ a string ΟΔβÄîβÄΙ a character ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Performs a case-sensitive search for the character in the string ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Returns TRUE if the character occurs in the string, otherwise returns FALSE Encrypt() ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Takes a password as a parameter of type string ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Returns the encrypted form of the password as a string Decrypt() ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Takes an encrypted password as a parameter of type string ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Returns the decrypted form of the password as a string Note: in a case-sensitive comparison, 'a' is not the same as 'A'. 15 9618/22/M/J/22 [Turn over (a) Write pseudocode for the module Exists() . [5] 16 9618/22/M/J/22 (b) A new module SearchDuplicates() will: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ search for the first password that occurs more than once in the array and output a message each time a duplicate is found. For example, if the same password was used for the three websites ThisWebsite.com, website27.net and websiteZ99.org, then the following messages will be output: "Password for ThisWebsite.com also used for website27.net" "Password for ThisWebsite.com also used for websiteZ99.org" ΟΔβ²§¬Δ end once all messages have been output. The module will output a message if no duplicates are found. For example: "No duplicate passwords found" Write efficient pseudocode for the module SearchDuplicates() . Encrypt() and Decrypt() functions have been written. Note : It is necessary to decrypt each password before checking its value. 17 9618/22/M/J/22 [Turn over [8] 18 9618/22/M/J/22 (c) A password has a fixed format, consisting of three groups of four alphanumeric characters, separated by the hyphen character '-'. An example of a password is: "FxAf-3haV-Tq49" Each password must: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ be 14 characters long ΟΔβ²§¬Δ be organised as three groups of four alphanumeric characters. The groups are separated by hyphen characters ΟΔβ²§¬Δ not include any duplicated characters, except for the hyphen characters. An algorithm is needed for a new function GeneratePassword() , which will generate and return a password in this format. Assume that the following modules have already been written:

8(a) FUNCTION Exists(ThisString : STRING, Search : CHAR) RETURNS BOOLEAN DECLARE Found : BOOLEAN DECLARE Index : INTEGER Found Ο·βÄö¬§ FALSE Index Ο·βÄö¬§ 1 WHILE Found = FALSE AND Index <= LENGTH(ThisString) IF MID(ThisString, Index, 1) = Search THEN Found Ο·βÄö¬§ TRUE ELSE Index Ο·βÄö¬§ Index + 1 ENDIF ENDWHILE RETURN Found ENDFUNCTION Marks as follows (Conditional loop solution): 1 Conditional loop while character not found and not end of string 2 Extract a char in a loop 3 Compare with parameter without case conversion in a loop 4 If match found, set termination logic in a loop 5 Return BOOLEAN value ALTERNATIVE (Using Count-controlled loop): FOR Index Ο·βÄö¬§ 1 TO LENGTH(ThisString) IF MID(ThisString, Index, 1) = Search THEN RETURN TRUE ENDIF NEXT Index RETURN FALSE Marks as follows (Count-controlled loop variant): 1 Loop for length of ThisString (allow from 0 or 1) 2 Extract a char in a loop 3 Compare with parameter without case conversion in a loop 4 If match found, immediate RETURN of TRUE 5 Return FALSE after the loop // Return Boolean if no immediate RETURN 5 9618/22 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 13 of 14 Question Answer Marks

8(b) PROCEDURE SearchDuplicates() DECLARE IndexA, IndexB : INTEGER DECLARE ThisPassword, ThisValue : STRING DECLARE Duplicates : BOOLEAN Duplicates Ο·βÄö¬§ FALSE IndexA Ο·βÄö¬§ 1 WHILE Duplicates = FALSE AND IndexA < 500 ThisValue Ο·βÄö¬§ Secret[IndexA, 2] IF ThisValue <> "" THEN ThisPassword Ο·βÄö¬§ Decrypt(ThisValue) FOR IndexB Ο·βÄö¬§ IndexA + 1 TO 500 // IF Secret[IndexB, 2] <> "" THEN IF Decrypt(Secret[IndexB, 2]) = ThisPassword THEN OUTPUT "Password for " & Secret[IndexA, 1] & "also used for " & Secret[IndexB, 1] Duplicates Ο·βÄö¬§ TRUE ENDIF ENDIF NEXT IndexB ENDIF IndexA Ο·βÄö¬§ IndexA + 1 ENDWHILE IF Duplicates = FALSE THEN OUTPUT "No duplicate passwords found" ENDIF ENDPROCEDURE Marks as follows to Max 8 : 1. (Any) conditional loop... 2. ... from 1 to 499 while (attempt at) no duplicate 3. Skip unused password 4. Use Decrypt() and assign return value to ThisPassword 5. If match found, set outer loop termination 8. and attempt an Output message giving duplicate 9. Output 'No duplicate passwords found' message if no duplicates found after the loop 8 9618/22 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 14 of 14 Question Answer Marks

question_text: A program allows a user to save passwords used to log in to websites. A stored password is then inserted automatically when the user logs in to the corresponding website. A student is developing a program to generate a strong password. The password will be of a fixed format, consisting of three groups of four alphanumeric characters, separated by the hyphen character '-'. An example of a password is: "FxAf-3hzV-Aq49" A valid password: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ must be 14 characters long ΟΔβ²§¬Δ must be organised as three groups of four alphanumeric characters. The groups are separated by hyphen characters ΟΔβ²§¬Δ may include duplicated characters, provided these appear in different groups. The programmer has started to define program modules as follows: Module Description RandomChar() ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Generates a single random character from within one of the following ranges: ΟΔβÄîβÄΙ 'a' to 'z' ΟΔβÄîβÄΙ 'A' to 'Z' ΟΔβÄîβÄΙ '0' to '9' ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Returns the character Exists() ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Takes two parameters: ΟΔβÄîβÄΙ a string ΟΔβÄîβÄΙ a character ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Performs a case-sensitive search for the character in the string ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Returns TRUE if the character occurs in the string, otherwise returns FALSE Generate() ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Generates a valid password ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Uses RandomChar() and Exists() ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Returns the password Note: in a case-sensitive comparison, 'a' is not the same as 'A'. 15 9618/23/M/J/22 [Turn over (a) Write pseudocode for the module Generate() . [7] 16 9618/23/M/J/22 (b) A global 2D array Secret of type STRING stores the passwords together with the website domain name where they are used. Secret contains 1000 elements organised as 500 rows by 2 columns. Unused elements contain the empty string ( "" ). These may occur anywhere in the array. An example of part of the array is: Array element Value Secret[27, 1] " Secret[27, 2] "ΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬è" Secret[28, 1] " Secret[28, 2] "ΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬è" Note: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ For security, the passwords are stored in an encrypted form, shown as "ΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬èΟΔβÄî¬è" in the example. ΟΔβ²§¬Δ The passwords cannot be used without being decrypted. ΟΔβ²§¬Δ You may assume that the encrypted form of a password will not be an empty string. Additional modules are defined as follows: Module Description Encrypt() ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Takes a password as a string ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Returns the encrypted form of the password as a string Decrypt() ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Takes an encrypted password as a string ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Returns the decrypted form of the password as a string FindPassword() ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Takes a website domain name as a string ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Searches for the website domain name in the array Secret ΟΔβ²§¬Δ If the website domain name is found, the decrypted password is returned ΟΔβ²§¬Δ If the website domain name is not found, an empty string is returned AddPassword() ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Takes two parameters as strings: a website domain name and a password ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Searches for the website domain name in the array Secret and if not found, adds the website domain name and the encrypted password to the array ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Returns TRUE if the website domain name and encrypted password are added to the array, otherwise returns FALSE The first three modules have been written. 17 9618/23/M/J/22 [Turn over Write pseudoc
mark_scheme_text:
9(a) FUNCTION Generate() RETURNS STRING DECLARE Password, Group : STRING DECLARE NextChar : CHAR DECLARE ACount, BCount : INTEGER CONSTANT HYPHEN = '-' Password Ο·βÄö¬§ "" FOR ACount Ο·βÄö¬§ 1 TO 3 Group Ο·βÄö¬§ "" FOR BCount Ο·βÄö¬§ 1 TO 4 REPEAT NextChar Ο·βÄö¬§ RandomChar() UNTIL Exists(Group, NextChar) = FALSE Group Ο·βÄö¬§ Group & NextChar NEXT BCount Password Ο·βÄö¬§ Password & Group & HYPHEN NEXT ACount Password Ο·βÄö¬§ LEFT(Password, 14) // remove final hyphen RETURN Password ENDFUNCTION Marks as follows to Max 7 : 1 Declaration and initialisation of Password as STRING 2 Outer loop for three groups / until password is complete // three group loops 3 Attempt to use of both RandomChar() and Exists() in a loop 4 (Inner) loop for 4 characters in a group // note every 4 chars in a loop 5 Conditional loop until char is unique 6 Concatenating unique character to Group in a loop 7 Concatenate Group / random character to Password in a loop 8 (Attempt to) insert hyphens between groups (or removing later) and Return Password 7 9618/23 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 11 of 11 Question Answer Marks

9(b) FUNCTION AddPassword(Name, Password : STRING) RETURNS BOOLEAN DECLARE Index : INTEGER DECLARE Added : BOOLEAN Added Ο·βÄö¬§ FALSE Index Ο·βÄö¬§ 1 IF FindPassword(Name) = "" THEN // Domain name not in // array WHILE Added = FALSE AND Index <= 500 IF Secret[Index, 1] = "" THEN Secret[Index, 1] Ο·βÄö¬§ Name Secret[Index, 2] Ο·βÄö¬§ Encrypt(Password) Added Ο·βÄö¬§ TRUE ELSE Index Ο·βÄö¬§ Index + 1 ENDIF ENDWHILE ENDIF RETURN Added ENDFUNCTION Marks as follows: 1 Check that the website domain name isn't already in array using FindPassword() / linear search, otherwise: 2 (Conditional) loop while not added and not end of array 3 Check for unused element by testing value in column 1 in a loop 4 If unused, write parameter values to column 1 and 2 and set flag / variable 5 ...having used Encrypt() on the password 6 Return BOOLEAN value (correctly in all cases) 6

9(c) One mark per point to Max 3 . Solution based on field length: Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Convert the length of the website domain name (either field) ΟΔβ²§¬Π Ο·βÄö¬Ζ ΟΔβ²§¬Π to a string of fixed length Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Form a string by concatenate this string with the other two (and write as one line of the file) Solution based on use of separator character: Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Select a (separator) character that cannot occur in the domain name (e.g. space) Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Create a string from the domain name followed by the separator Ο·βÄö¬Ζ ...Concatenate the encrypted password (and write as one line of the file) 3

question_text: Reduced Instruction Set Computers (RISC) and Complex Instruction Set Computers (CISC) are two types of processor. (a) Describe what is meant by RISC and CISC processors . RISC CISC [4] (b) Identify two differences between RISC and CISC processors. 1 2 [2] 8 9618/31/M/J/22

4(a) RISC max 2 any two from: Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Uses simple instructions Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Uses fixed length instructions Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Instructions only require one clock cycle Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Uses many registers Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Makes use of pipelining Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Hardwired CU CISC max 2 any two from: Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Uses many instruction formats Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Uses variable length instructions Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Makes use of different addressing modes Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Uses few registers Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Has a large instruction set Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Requires complex circuits Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Frequently uses cache Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Instructions (converted to sub-instructions that) may require many clock cycles Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Programmable CU 4

4(b) One mark for each difference max 2 from: Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC has fewer instructions // CISC has more instructions Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC has many registers // CISC has few registers Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISCΟΔβ²§β³Δs instructions are simpler // CISCΟΔβ²§β³Δs instructions are more complex Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC has a few instruction formats / CISC has many instruction formats Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC usually uses single-cycle instructions// CISC uses multi-cycle instructions Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC uses fixed-length instructions // CISC uses variable-length instructions Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC has better pipelineability // CISC has poorer pipelineability Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC requires less complex circuits// CISC requires more complex circuits Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC has fewer addressing modes // CISC has more addressing modes Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC makes more use of RAM// CISC makes more use of cache/less use of RAM Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC has a hard-wired control unit // CISC has a programmable control unit Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC only uses load and store instructions to address memory // CISC has many types of instructions to address memory 2 9618/31 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 7 of 11 Question Answer Marks

9(b) Instruction Label Opcode Operand LDM #20 STO Twenty LDI Y ADD Twenty STO Z Twenty: #20 Y: Z: One mark for LDM #20 seen One mark for storing 20 at any address One mark for labelling that address e.g. Twenty away from the program code One mark for labelling addresses away from the program code as Y and Z One mark for correct use of LDI Y One mark for correct use of STO Z One mark for correct use of ADD with labelled address 7

subject: A-Level Computer Science
question_text: Data types can be defined using pseudocode. The data type, BuildingRecord , is defined in pseudocode as: TYPE BuildingRecord DECLARE BuildingID : INTEGER DECLARE BuildingGroup : STRING DECLARE OwnerName : STRING DECLARE BuildingAddress : STRING DECLARE DateLastSold : DATE DECLARE PriceLastSold : REAL ENDTYPE A variable, BuildingRegister , is declared in pseudocode as: DECLARE BuildingRegister : BuildingRecord (a) Write pseudocode statements to assign: ΟΔβ²§¬Δ 1067 to the BuildingID of BuildingRegister ΟΔβ²§¬Δ house to the BuildingGroup of BuildingRegister [2] (b) The type definition for BuildingRecord is changed. The data type for BuildingGroup is changed to an enumerated type, BuildingType , with values of house, bungalow, apartment and farm. (i) Write the type declaration for BuildingType in pseudocode . [2] (ii) Write the new declaration for BuildingGroup in pseudocode . [1] (iii) Write the new pseudocode statement to assign house to BuildingGroup of BuildingRegister . [1] 3 9618/32/M/J/22 [Turn over (c) The program is to be rewritten using Object-Oriented Programming (OOP). The data type BuildingRecord is to be changed to a class, BuildingClass . The properties for BuildingClass are BuildingID , BuildingGroup , OwnerName , BuildingAddress , DateLastSold and PriceLastSold . All the properties are set to PRIVATE , for example: PRIVATE PriceLastSold : REAL (i) Write the declaration in pseudocode for OwnerName as PRIVATE . subject: A-Level Computer Science
question_text: A digital signature is used to validate the authenticity of an electronic message. In order to produce a digital signature, a digital certificate is required. (a) State how a digital certificate is obtained. [3] (b) (i) Explain how a digital signature is produced before the message is sent. [3] (ii) Explain how the digital signature can be checked on receipt to ensure that the message has not been altered during transmission. [4] 11 9618/32/M/J/22 [Turn over
mark_scheme_text:
7(a) Any three from MP1 enquiry made to Certificate Authority (CA) MP2 enquirerΟΔβ²§β³Δs details checked by CA MP3 if enquirer details verified by CA then public key is agreed MP4 CA creates/issues certificate that includes the enquirers public key MP5 encrypting data sent to/by CA with the CAΟΔβ²§β³Δs public/private key 3

7(b)(i) MP1 The message is hashed with (the agreed hashing algorithm)ΟΔβ²§¬Π MP2 ΟΔβ²§¬Π to produce a message digest MP3 The message digest is then encrypted with the senderΟΔβ²§β³Δs private key to form the digital signature 3

7(b)(ii) Any four from MP1 The message together with the digital signature is decrypted using the receiverΟΔβ²§β³Δs private key MP2 The digital signature received is decrypted with the senderΟΔβ²§β³Δs public key to recover the message digest sent MP3 The decrypted message received is hashed with the agreed hashing algorithm to reproduce the message digest of the message received MP4 The two message digests are compared MP5 ΟΔβ²§¬Π if they are the same the message has not been altered // if they are different the message has been altered 4 Question Answer Marks

question_text: A binary search or a linear search can be used to look for a specific value in an array. (a) Complete this pseudocode algorithm for a linear search. DECLARE MyList : ARRAY[0:9] OF INTEGER DECLARE MaxIndex : INTEGER DECLARE Index : INTEGER DECLARE Found : BOOLEAN DECLARE ValueToFind : INPUT ValueToFind Found ΟΔβÄ†¬ê FALSE Index ΟΔβÄ†¬ê
mark_scheme_text:
8(a) INTEGER 9 // LENGTH(MyList) - 1 Index + 1 "Value not found" (or any similar phrase) 4

8(b)(i) The list to be searched must be ordered/sorted 1 9618/32 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 9 of 9 Question Answer Marks

8(b)(ii) Any four from MP1 Find the middle item / index MP2 Check the value of middle item in the list to be searched MP3 If equal item searched for is found MP4 If this is not equal/greater/less than the item searched for MP5 ΟΔβ²§¬Π discard the half of the list that does not contain the search item MP6 Repeat the above steps until the item searched for is found MP7 ΟΔβ²§¬Π or there is only one item left in the list and it is not the item searched for // lower bound > / = upper bound 4

8(b)(iii) As the number of items in the list increases the time to search the list increases 1

8(c) MP1 Linear search O(n) and Binary search O(log 2 n) / O(Log n) MP2 time to search increases linearly in relation to the number of items in the list for a linear search and logarithmically for a Binary search MP3 time to search increases less rapidly for a binary search and time to search increases more rapidly for a linear search 3 Question Answer Marks 9 To trap (some) runtime errors To prevent a program halting unexpectedly To produce meaningful error messages for these errors Example divide by zero // end of file // file not found 4

subject: A-Level Computer Science
question_text: Reduced Instruction Set Computers (RISC) and Complex Instruction Set Computers (CISC) are two types of processor. (a) Describe what is meant by RISC and CISC processors . RISC CISC [4] (b) Identify two differences between RISC and CISC processors. 1 2 [2] 8 9618/33/M/J/22
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) RISC max 2 any two from: Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Uses simple instructions Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Uses fixed length instructions Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Instructions only require one clock cycle Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Uses many registers Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Makes use of pipelining Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Hardwired CU CISC max 2 any two from: Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Uses many instruction formats Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Uses variable length instructions Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Makes use of different addressing modes Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Uses few registers Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Has a large instruction set Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Requires complex circuits Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Frequently uses cache Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Instructions (converted to sub-instructions that) may require many clock cycles Ο·βÄö¬Ζ Programmable CU 4

4(b) One mark for each difference max 2 from: Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC has fewer instructions // CISC has more instructions Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC has many registers // CISC has few registers Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISCΟΔβ²§β³Δs instructions are simpler // CISCΟΔβ²§β³Δs instructions are more complex Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC has a few instruction formats / CISC has many instruction formats Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC usually uses single-cycle instructions// CISC uses multi-cycle instructions Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC uses fixed-length instructions // CISC uses variable-length instructions Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC has better pipelineability // CISC has poorer pipelineability Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC requires less complex circuits// CISC requires more complex circuits Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC has fewer addressing modes // CISC has more addressing modes Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC makes more use of RAM// CISC makes more use of cache/less use of RAM Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC has a hard-wired control unit // CISC has a programmable control unit Ο·βÄö¬Ζ RISC only uses load and store instructions to address memory // CISC has many types of instructions to address memory 2 9618/33 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 7 of 11 Question Answer Marks

question_text: State two benefits to a programmer of using Dynamic Link Library (DLL) files. 1 2 [2] (b) Memory management is one of the tasks performed by an Operating System (OS). Describe the ways in which memory management organises and allocates Random Access Memory (RAM). [2] (c) An Operating System may include a utility program to compress text files. Describe one appropriate method of compressing a text file. [3] (d) Explain the reasons why increasing the amount of cache memory can improve the performance of a CPU. [2] (e) State the name of a peripheral device port that provides a physical connection in the computer for each of these peripherals. 3D printer Monitor [2] 19 9618/11/O/N/22
mark_scheme_text:
7(a) 1 mark for each benefit ( max 2 ): ΟΔβ²§¬Δ (main) memory requirements for program are reduced as dynamic link library is loaded only once / when required ΟΔβ²§¬Δ the executable file size is smaller because the executable does not contain all the library routines ΟΔβ²§¬Δ maintenance not needed to be done by the programmer because the DLL is separate from program ΟΔβ²§¬Δ no need to recompile the main program when changes are made to DLL because changes / improvements/ error correction to the DLL file code are done independ ently of the main program 2

7(b) 1 mark for each bullet point ( max 2 ): ΟΔβ²§¬Δ RAM is assigned into blocks ΟΔβ²§¬Δ dynamic allocation of RAM to programs / processes ΟΔβ²§¬Δ reclaims unused blocks of RAM ΟΔβ²§¬Δ prevents two programs / processes occupying the same area of RAM at the same time ΟΔβ²§¬Δ moves data from secondary storage when needed // manages paging, segmentation and virtual memory 2 9618 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 20 22 22 Page 10 of 11 Question Answer Marks

7(c) 1 mark for each bullet point ( max 3 ): ΟΔβ²§¬Δ lossless compression ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Run Length Encoding ΟΔβ²§¬Δ repeated sequences of characters are replaced by ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ... a single copy of the character ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ... and a counter of the number of characters 3

7(d) 1 mark for each bullet point ( max 2 ): ΟΔβ²§¬Δ cache is fast access memory (close to the CPU) ΟΔβ²§¬Δ cache stores frequently used instructions / data ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ... more cache means more instructions / data can be transferred faster ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ... less swapping between RAM and cache ΟΔβ²§¬Δ prevents the CPU idling while waiting for data 2

7(e) 1 mark for each device. ΟΔβ²§¬Δ 3D printer : USB port / COM port ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Monitor : HDMI / VGA / USB / DisplayPort 2 Question Answer Marks 8 1 mark for bullet point ( max 4 ): ΟΔβ²§¬Δ CSMA/CD is a protocol used to detect and prevent collisions in a bus topology ΟΔβ²§¬Δ before transmitting, a device checks if the channel is busy ΟΔβ²§¬Δ If the channel is busy the device waits // if the channel is free the data is sent ΟΔβ²§¬Δ because there is more than one computer connected to the same transmission medium ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ... two workstations can start to transmit at the same time, causing a collision ΟΔβ²§¬Δ If a collision is detected by the device, transmission is aborted / a jamming signal is transmitted ΟΔβ²§¬Δ both devices wait a (different) random time and then try again 4 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Computer Science
question_text: The following table shows part of the instruction set for a processor. The processor has one general purpose register, the Accumulator (ACC), and an Index Register (IX). Instruction Explanation Opcode Operand LDM #n Immediate addressing. Load the number n to ACC LDD <address> Direct addressing. Load the contents of the location at the given address to ACC LDX <address> Indexed addressing. Form the address from <address> + the contents of the index register. Copy the contents of this calculated address to ACC LDR #n Immediate addressing. Load the number n to IX STO <address> Store the contents of ACC at the given address ADD <address> Add the contents of the given address to the ACC ADD #n Add the denary number n to the ACC INC <register> Add 1 to the contents of the register (ACC or IX) JMP <address> Jump to the given address CMP <address> Compare the contents of ACC with the contents of <address> CMI <address> Indirect addressing. The address to be used is at the given address. Compare the contents of ACC with the contents of this second address JPE <address> Following a compare instruction, jump to <address> if the compare was True JPN <address> Following a compare instruction, jump to <address> if the compare was False END Return control to the operating system <address> can be an absolute or symbolic address # denotes a denary number, e.g. #123 B denotes a binary number, e.g. B01001101 11 9618/12/O/N/22 [Turn over (a) Trace the program currently in memory using the trace table, stopping when line 90 is executed for a second time. Address Instruction

question_text: Explain the need for scheduling in process management. [3] (b) Describe these scheduling routines and identify a benefit for each one. Shortest job first Round robin First come first served [6] 9 9618/32/O/N/22 [Turn over
mark_scheme_text:
9(a) One mark for each point ( Max 3 ) ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Process scheduling allows more than one program/task to appear to be executed at the same time / enables multi - tasking / multiprogramming. ΟΔβ²§¬Δ To allow high priority jobs to be completed first. ΟΔβ²§¬Δ To keep the CPU busy all the time ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π to ensure that all processes execute efficiently ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π and to have reduced wait times for all processes / to ensure all processes have fair access to the CPU / prevent starvation of some processes. 3 9618 / 32 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 20 22 22 Page 13 of 16 Question Answer Marks

9(b) One mark for each point ( Max 2 ) for Shortest job first : ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Process are executed in ascending order of the amount of CPU time required // Short processes are executed first and followed by longer processes. ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Πwhich leads to an increased throughput (because more processes can be executed in a smaller amount of time). One mark for each point ( Max 2 ) for Round robin : ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Each process is served by the CPU for a fixed time/time slice (so all processes are given the same priority). ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Starvation doesnΟΔβ²§β³Δt occur (because for each round robin cycle, every process is given a fixed time/time slice to execute). One mark for each point ( Max 2 ) for First come first served : ΟΔβ²§¬Δ No complex logic, each process request is queued as it is received and executed one by one. ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Starvation doesnΟΔβ²§β³Δt occur (because every process will eventually get a ch ance to run) // less processor overhead. 6 Question Answer Marks

ΟΔβ²§¬Δ The message is decrypted using the recipientΟΔβ²§β³Δs private key. // The message is decrypted using the senderΟΔβ²§β³Δs public k ey. 2

6(c) One mark for each correct point ( Max 4 ) ΟΔβ²§¬Δ The message together with the digital signature is decrypted using the receiverΟΔβ²§β³Δs private key ΟΔβ²§¬Δ The digital signature received is decrypted with the senderΟΔβ²§β³Δs public key to recover the message digest sent ΟΔβ²§¬Δ The decrypted message received is hashed with the agreed hashing algorithm to reproduce the message digest of the message received ΟΔβ²§¬Δ The two message digests are compared ΟΔβ²§¬Δ ΟΔβ²§¬Π if both digests are the same the message has not been altered // if they are diff erent the message has been altered. 4 9618 / 33 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 20 22 22 Page 9 of 15 Question Answer Marks

mark_scheme_text:
12(a) One mark for each point ( Max 6 ) ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Initialisation of upper bound ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Test if upper bound is less than lower bound ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Re - setting of mid value if current value is lower than the target ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Re - setting of mid value if current value is higher than the target ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Finding the value ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Correct termination of loop Lower Ο·βÄö¬§ 0 Upper Ο·βÄö¬§ 99 Mid Ο·βÄö¬§ 0 Exit Ο·βÄö¬§ FALSE OUTPUT "Enter the name to be found " INPUT Target REPEAT IF Upper < Lower THEN OUTPUT Target, " does not exist" Exit Ο·βÄö¬§ TRUE ENDIF Mid Ο·βÄö¬§ Lower + (Upper ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Lower + 1) DIV 2 IF Names[Mid] < Target THEN Lower Ο·βÄö¬§ Mid + 1 ENDIF IF Names[Mid] > Target THEN Upper Ο·βÄö¬§ Mid - 1 ENDIF IF Names[Mid] = Target THEN OUTPUT Target, " was found at location ", Mid Exit Ο·βÄö¬§ TRUE ENDIF UNTIL Exit // UNTIL Exit = TRUE 6

12(b)(i) O(n) 1 9618 / 33 Cambridge International AS & A Level ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 20 22 22 Page 15 of 15 Question Answer Marks
